retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Catheter (Archived) -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural catheters are placed within the epidural space. The epidural space exists circumferentially between the dura mater and the ligamentum flavum, extending from the foramen magnum to the sacral hiatus. Within the epidural space lies epidural veins, fat, lymphatics, and nerve roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Saddle Anesthetic Block -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is a potential, low-pressure space between the dura and bony vertebral canal called the extradural or epidural space filled by areolar tissue, fat, lymphatics, arteries, and internal vertebral venous plexus. A potential subdural space is between the dura and the arachnoid mater. The dura forms a sac below the cord that ends at the second sacral segment (S2) level and extends along each nerve root shortly, becoming continuous with the epineurium of spinal nerves. The subarachnoid space between the arachnoid and pia mater contains the cerebrospinal fluid, the site for injecting various anesthetic drugs. The subarachnoid space extends laterally along with nerve roots to the dorsal root ganglia. It ends at the level of S2 in adults but extends lower in children. An essential landmark while identifying the correct vertebral space is the Tuffier line, also known as an intercristal line, an imaginary plane joining the iliac crests of both sides. It approximately identifies the spinous process of the fourth lumbar (L4) vertebra. See Image. Spinal Cord, Neurology.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Catheter (Archived) -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Surface landmarks can be used to approximate spinal levels. The nipple line approximates T4, the inferior border of the scapula approximates T7, and the superior aspect of the iliac crests approximates L4. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Anesthesia -- Anatomy and Physiology -- The Spinal Cord and Epidural Space
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal epidural space extends from the foramen magnum at the base of the skull to the sacral hiatus and contains fatty and connective tissues, blood vessels, and lymphatics. The blood vessels may dilate in the presence of ascites or during pregnancy, increasing the risk of a traumatic or bloody puncture. The epidural space is further divided into the anterior and posterior epidural spaces. The anterior epidural space contains the ventral spinal nerves, the basivertebral veins, and the internal vertebral venous plexus. The posterior epidural space contains the dorsal spinal nerves, the intervertebral veins, and the sinuvertebral nerves. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Veins -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of a spinal epidural abscess usually results in congestion within the internal vertebral venous plexus. This enlarged venous plexus can mechanically compress the spinal cord resulting in myelopathy. Sepsis can also occur by way of the vertebral plexus. Also, since direct connections exist between the pelvis, the Batson plexus, and cranial venous sinuses, emboli and malignancies can disseminate via this hematogenous route. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Blood Patch -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The epidural space is bounded by the dural meninges anteriorly, the ligamentum flavum posteriorly and the sides of the vertebral walls laterally. It extends from the foramen magnum to the sacral hiatus. Its contents are tiny arteries and lymphatics, epidural fat, and a network of small valveless veins (Batson plexus). The epidural "space" is more of a potential space; a series of discontinuous compartments that open up when injected with liquid or air. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Vertebral Canal Blood Supply -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: During pregnancy, the extradural venous plexus can become engorged when the gravid uterus lay supine and restricts blood supply to the inferior vena cava. The blood would then shunt to the external venous plexus. Because of the increasing supply of blood to the area, some studies claim that fewer spinal anesthetics would be necessary during pregnancy. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Meninges -- Structure and Function -- The Epidural Space
retrieved_docs.content: Between the ligamentum flavum and the superficial dura mater is the spinal epidural space. This location contains various structures such as adipose tissue, connective tissue, nerves, the internal vertebral venous plexus, and lymphatics. It is a site commonly used for analgesic purposes and is a potential site for infection. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Veins -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The position, course, and anastomotic pattern of the posterior spinal veins show considerable variability. It is usually a single vessel near the conus, but it can be paired or form a plexus called the posterior venous plexus. [3] Truncular type drainage exists in 80% of cases and plexiform type drainage 20% of cases. Variation of radicular veins: While 60% of radicular veins exit the dura with the nerve root, 40% exit through different foramina between adjacent spinal nerves. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Stimulation -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Venous supply: There is a vertebral venous plexus that drains into the vertebral canal. These veins empty into the azygos vein that ultimately empties into the superior vena cava (SVC). This plexus is of particular importance in patients with masses or increased intraabdominal pressure compressing the SVC. When this occurs, there is a backup of blood into the epidural space, which increased the risk of cannulating the veins with the epidural needle. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Catheter (Archived) -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The spine consists of 33 bony vertebrae. This includes 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, and 4 coccygeal. The spinal cord runs through the vertebral canal, extending from the foramen magnum to roughly the L1 level in most adults. It is surrounded by three meningeal layers: the pia, arachnoid, and dura. Nerve roots emerge bilaterally at each vertebral level to innervate their respective dermatomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient-Controlled Analgesia -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Modalities for PCA administration include intravenous lines, central lines, epidural catheters, peripheral nerve catheters, or transdermal delivery systems. Any peripheral vein can be used to insert a catheter and begin the administration of PCA. Central lines are placed in the internal jugular, subclavian, or femoral veins. [5] Epidural catheter placement is in the epidural space between the dura and the ligamentum flavum, which lines the anterior portions of the vertebral lamina. Within the epidural space sits spinal nerves, blood vessels, and fat. [6] Indwelling epidural catheter placement is at thoracic and lumbar spinal levels. Peripheral nerve catheters are placed adjacently to the nerve or plexus and intended to block continuously. Depending on the desired target location, these run through the skin and potentially muscle. [2] Transdermal PCA delivery is through a fentanyl iontophoretic transdermal system, which is placed on the epidermis and provides a dose of fentanyl when the patient pushes a button on the patch. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Vertebral Canal Blood Supply -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord drains via the single anterior spinal vein and single posterior spinal vein which in turn drain into the internal vertebral venous plexus located in the epidural space within the extradural fat. [2] These veins eventually empty into the external vertebral venous plexus via the basivertebral veins. From the external venous plexus, blood drains systemically depending on the location; for example, the thoracic region of the spinal cord empties into the azygos system. Of note, the Batson venous plexus (which drains many organs in the pelvis region including bladder, prostate, and rectum) feeds into the internal vertebral plexus, and the Batson plexus does not contain valves. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Veins -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The vertebral venous plexus, also called the Batson plexus refers to veins located extradurally within the spinal canal (internal plexus), surrounding the vertebral column (external plexus) as well as horizontal basivertebral veins that run through the vertebral bodies (basivertebral plexus). [1] [2] These veins comprise the extradural spinal venous system and drain both the spinal cord and the vertebral column. The internal vertebral plexus is made up of two anterior longitudinal veins coursing on either side of the posterior longitudinal ligament and two posterior veins coursing anterior to the ligamentum flavum. [3] The internal plexus communicates with various intracranial venous plexus. Intervertebral veins link the external and internal vertebral venous plexus, and via segmental veins, drains blood into the extraspinal system. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Veins -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Venous occlusion due to emboli or thrombus from sepsis or malignancy or occlusion of the vertebral venous plexus during decompression sickness can also cause ischemic myelopathy. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1232
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedure on vein is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the vén (vein), establishing the vein as the primary anatomical site of the operation. In addition, the vein serves as an indirect procedural site for إراقة الدم (blood‑drainage) procedures, indicating that while blood‑drainage interventions may not act directly on the vein, the vessel is nonetheless involved in the pathway of such treatments. This dual role of the vein—as a direct target for operative procedures and an indirect conduit for blood‑drainage techniques—underscores its central importance in vascular interventions and related patient care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: The internal rectal venous plexus serves as the anatomical location where hemorrhoids are identified, and it is also the operative site for the procedure known as ربط الباسور. Recognizing this dual role of the internal rectal venous plexus is important for clinicians diagnosing hemorrhoidal disease and for surgeons performing ربط الباسور, ensuring that interventions are accurately targeted to the appropriate vascular structures within the rectum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is a medical intervention that targets vascular structures and is categorized under the broader group of other procedures on vessels of the head and neck. Within this category, specific activities such as hemodynamic monitoring and vascular cannula insertion are recognized as distinct but related procedures. This hierarchical organization highlights that procedures on blood vessels encompass both monitoring of circulatory dynamics and the placement of vascular cannulas, and they are considered a subset of the larger collection of head and neck vascular procedures.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate that a wide range of interventional neurologic and vascular procedures—neurolytic sympathetic nerve injections, sympathetic nerve blocks, spinal catheter or stimulator insertions, and endovascular intracranial drug deliveries—share a common reliance on medicinal products. Within this product class, specific pharmacologic groups such as dopamine agonists and methamphetamine are recognized, and the products are further characterized by detailed component classifications. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in selecting appropriate agents, anticipating cross‑procedure drug interactions, and ensuring consistent documentation of therapeutic substances across multiple specialties.
